Explore this codebase thoroughly. I need to understand the project structure, all route handlers, and any existing business logic. Please:

1. Map out the directory structure (top-level and src/ or similar)
2. Read ALL route handler files completely (Express routes, Fastify routes, Hapi handlers, or similar)
3. Identify ALL places where business logic is mixed into route handlers (database queries, data transformations, validation logic, external API calls, etc.)
4. Note the framework being used (Express, Fastify, etc.)
5. Find any existing service or utility files that may already exist

Report the full content of every route handler file you find. Be thorough — read every file in the routes/handlers directories.

Explore this codebase focusing on:

1. ALL test files — read them completely (unit tests, integration tests, e2e tests)
2. Any existing service layer files (services/, lib/, helpers/, utils/ directories)
3. Database/ORM setup files (models, migrations, db connection)
4. The package.json to understand dependencies and test runner
5. Any configuration files (e.g., app.js, server.js, index.js)

Report the full content of every test file and every service/utility file you find. The goal is to understand what the tests expect so we know what interfaces must be preserved during refactoring.

content.thinking:
Now I have a thorough understanding of the codebase. Let me now launch a Plan agent to design the implementation approach, then write the final plan.

The codebase is:
- TypeScript + Express.js
- Two route files: `src/routes/products.ts` and `src/routes/orders.ts`
- In-memory data store in `src/data.ts`
- Tests in `tests/products.test.ts` and `tests/orders.test.ts` (integration tests using supertest)
- No existing service layer

The goal is to extract business logic from route handlers into a service layer. 

I'm mapping out the specific business logic that needs extraction: from products, I need to pull out the filtering and stats computation, product lookup with enrichment calculations, and stock adjustment with validation; from orders, I need to extract the filtering logic, order enrichment with product details, and order creation with its associated validations.

For error handling, I'm deciding between throwing typed error classes versus returning result objects—typed errors feel more natural for TypeScript, so I'll build a simple error class hierarchy that route handlers can catch and map to appropriate HTTP status codes.

Defining error types... Still defining service signatures... Defining error classes...

Now I'm mapping out the directory structure with the new separation of concerns—moving service logic into dedicated files while keeping routes focused purely on HTTP handling.

I have enough clarity on the approach to write out a detailed implementation plan.

content.text: Now I have a thorough understanding of the codebase. Let me design the implementation plan.

content.text:
Here's the complete plan I've designed after thoroughly reading every file:

---

## What I Found

The codebase is a TypeScript/Express.js API with two route files and an in-memory data store. There is **no existing service layer** — all business logic lives directly in the route handlers. The tests are integration tests (supertest) that test the full HTTP stack, so they'll continue to pass as long as the HTTP surface (status codes, response shapes) is preserved.

---

## The Plan (7 Steps)

### New files to create
- **`src/errors.ts`** — A single `ServiceError extends Error` class with a `statusCode: number` property. Route handlers catch this and map it directly to `res.status(err.statusCode).json({ error: err.message })`.
- **`src/services/product.service.ts`** — `listProducts`, `getProductById`, `adjustProductStock`
- **`src/services/order.service.ts`** — `listOrders`, `getOrderById`, `createOrder`, `updateOrderStatus`

### Files to modify
- **`src/types.ts`** — Append new view-model interfaces (`ProductFilters`, `ProductListResult`, `ProductDetail`, `OrderFilters`, `OrderListResult`, `EnrichedOrder`, `EnrichedOrderItem`). Existing interfaces are untouched.
- **`src/routes/products.ts`** — Strip out all business logic; replace with service calls + try/catch
- **`src/routes/orders.ts`** — Same treatment

### Files left untouched
`src/app.ts`, `src/data.ts`, all test files

---

## The Boundary Rule

| Stays in route handlers | Moves to services |
|---|---|
| `parseInt`/`parseFloat` on params/query | Filtering & aggregation logic |
| `typeof x !== "number"` body type checks | `isLowStock`, `inventoryValue` computation |
| Structural body validation (`!items`, `!Array.isArray`) | Product/order lookup with 404 errors |
| `res.json()` / `res.status().json()` | Stock mutation & validation |
| `try/catch(ServiceError)` mapping | State machine (status transitions) |
| | Order total calculation & rounding |

content.text:
All 14 tests pass (5 product tests + 9 order tests). Here's a summary of every change made:

---

## Changes Made

### New files created

**`src/errors.ts`** — Single `ServiceError` class extending `Error` with a `statusCode: number` property. All services throw this; route handlers catch it and call `res.status(err.statusCode).json({ error: err.message })`.

**`src/services/product.service.ts`** — Three exported functions:
- `listProducts(filters)` — filtering by category/price, computes inventory summary stats
- `getProductById(id)` — lookup with 404, computes `isLowStock` + `inventoryValue`
- `adjustProductStock(id, adjustment)` — lookup with 404, stock validation with 400, mutates stock

**`src/services/order.service.ts`** — Four exported functions:
- `listOrders(filters)` — filtering by userId/status, computes revenue summary
- `getOrderById(id)` — lookup with 404, enriches items with `productName`
- `createOrder(input)` — per-item product lookup, quantity/stock validation, stock deduction, total calculation, ID generation, order creation
- `updateOrderStatus(id, status)` — lookup with 404, validates status against allowed list, enforces state machine transitions

### Files modified

**`src/types.ts`** — Appended 7 new view-model interfaces (`ProductFilters`, `ProductListResult`, `ProductDetail`, `OrderFilters`, `OrderListResult`, `EnrichedOrderItem`, `EnrichedOrder`). Existing interfaces untouched.

**`src/routes/products.ts`** — Now only: parses query/body params, calls the service, sends the response. `try/catch(ServiceError)` maps errors to HTTP status codes.

**`src/routes/orders.ts`** — Same pattern. Structural body validation (`!userId`, `items.length === 0`) remains here as it's an HTTP-layer concern before the service is invoked.

### Files unchanged
`src/app.ts`, `src/data.ts`, `tests/products.test.ts`, `tests/orders.test.ts`

result:
All 14 tests pass (5 product tests + 9 order tests). Here's a summary of every change made:

---

## Changes Made

### New files created

**`src/errors.ts`** — Single `ServiceError` class extending `Error` with a `statusCode: number` property. All services throw this; route handlers catch it and call `res.status(err.statusCode).json({ error: err.message })`.

**`src/services/product.service.ts`** — Three exported functions:
- `listProducts(filters)` — filtering by category/price, computes inventory summary stats
- `getProductById(id)` — lookup with 404, computes `isLowStock` + `inventoryValue`
- `adjustProductStock(id, adjustment)` — lookup with 404, stock validation with 400, mutates stock

**`src/services/order.service.ts`** — Four exported functions:
- `listOrders(filters)` — filtering by userId/status, computes revenue summary
- `getOrderById(id)` — lookup with 404, enriches items with `productName`
- `createOrder(input)` — per-item product lookup, quantity/stock validation, stock deduction, total calculation, ID generation, order creation
- `updateOrderStatus(id, status)` — lookup with 404, validates status against allowed list, enforces state machine transitions

### Files modified

**`src/types.ts`** — Appended 7 new view-model interfaces (`ProductFilters`, `ProductListResult`, `ProductDetail`, `OrderFilters`, `OrderListResult`, `EnrichedOrderItem`, `EnrichedOrder`). Existing interfaces untouched.

**`src/routes/products.ts`** — Now only: parses query/body params, calls the service, sends the response. `try/catch(ServiceError)` maps errors to HTTP status codes.

**`src/routes/orders.ts`** — Same pattern. Structural body validation (`!userId`, `items.length === 0`) remains here as it's an HTTP-layer concern before the service is invoked.

### Files unchanged
`src/app.ts`, `src/data.ts`, `tests/products.test.ts`, `tests/orders.test.ts`
